title: Understanding the Methods Behind Tunnel Construction Under Mountains

lesson.learn: Mountain passageways, also known as tunnels, are complex engineering endeavors that facilitate safe and efficient travel through rugged terrain. The world's longest mountain tunnel, the Gotthard Base Tunnel in Switzerland, exemplifies this feat, spanning 57 kilometers and taking nearly two decades to complete (2015-2022). The tunnel's construction involved 3.8 million tons of excavated rock and the use of cutting-edge tunnel boring machines.
lesson.simpler: Compared to a simple water supply pipe, mountain tunnels are more intricate systems that require precise engineering to withstand immense pressure and stress. Imagine a massive pipe that must accommodate cars, trains, or people on foot while navigating through solid rock formations.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Key to stable tunnel construction lies in geotechnical understanding, material selection, and meticulous design. By analyzing rock formations and exploiting the right materials, engineers can create safe and durable passages under mountains.
lesson.deeperDive: Advancements in tunnel boring technology have significantly improved the efficiency and accuracy of large-scale tunnel projects. The Seikan Tunnel in Japan, for instance, has an impressive 53.8 kilometers of underwater passage that serves as a testament to modern tunnel engineering. Similarly, the Channel Tunnel between England and France has been upgraded to accommodate high-speed trains, reducing travel time between the two countries.
lesson.funFact: Despite advances in tunnel engineering, unexpected geological events like subsidence or rockfalls can still occur. The Channel Tunnel, for example, has experienced such incidents, highlighting the importance of continued research and monitoring.

people: * Karl von Terzaghi - founder of modern soil mechanics
* Othmar Ammann - Swiss-American engineer who designed iconic bridges and tunnels
* Charles Blndon - British engineer responsible for many UK tunnel projects
places: * Gotthard Base Tunnel, Switzerland - longest railway tunnel in the world
* Channel Tunnel, England/France - connecting Europe through the English Channel
* Seikan Tunnel, Japan - longest underwater railway tunnel
years: * 1819: Construction of the London and Birmingham Railway began, featuring several early tunnels under mountains
* 1873: Completion of the Gothard Railway in Switzerland marked a milestone in trans-Alpine tunnel engineering
* 1994: The Channel Tunnel between England and France was officially opened for rail traffic
